dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to 2123 Redacted for a priority one unconscious male suspected heroin overdose.
Ma'am, I'm Jordan, an EMT. Can you tell me what occurred before you called 911?
My boyfriend was in the car, unconscious. I think it's from heroin.
Understood. We'll follow up with the RFD team and begin assessments now.
Is the patient responding to any form of stimulation at all?
Eyes are closed, no spontaneous eye opening observed.
He has a Glasgow Coma Scale of 3-he's not reacting to verbal or painful stimuli.
RFD team states they started BVM ventilation upon finding him apneic and weak breathing. Is that still in use?
They are using the BVM at 15 liters per minute to help him breathe for now.
And is he breathing on his own at all now?
About 4 times a minute, but they think it's not enough on its own yet.
We'll keep the BVM going until his breathing stabilizes on its own. NPA already in place from RFD.
How is the breath sound now and what's his respiratory rate?
Rate is 16, shallow, and breath sounds are equal and clear bilaterally on auscultation.
What was the room air SpO2 before the BVM, and now with support?
Pre-BVM Sat was 50%, now it's 89% with manual ventilations on BVM at 15LPM.
We have a strong and regular radial pulse. No signs of external bleeding observed yet.
Skin is pale, warm, and diaphoretic. No signs of shock so far.
His blood pressure is 125/69 and appears stable at the moment.
Does he have any known medical or surgical history we should be aware of?
No, he only uses heroin occasionally. Never reported a chronic illness or allergies.
Can you approximate when he last used the drug or had something to eat?
He snorted something earlier but doesn't remember the exact time. He had no food before it happened, as far as I know.
Any symptoms other than unconsciousness or dizziness, nausea, or chest pain since then?
He was just very sleepy before this but no other symptoms like pain or vomiting.
On a scale of 0 to 10, is he experiencing pain of any sort at the moment?
He is denying any pain entirely. Pain Level is 0 right now.
Skin examination shows no rashes, scratches, or localized trauma at this time.
Pupils are noted as pinpoint bilaterally.
Did the patient report any chest or lung-related complaints since the incident?
No chest pain or breathing difficulty reported. 4-lead ECG has sinus tachycardia and no signs of acute MI or arrhythmia otherwise.
John, can you tell me what year it is? Let's assess your level of awareness now that you're more alert.
2020, I think. It's after October?
What city are we in right now and your full name, if you can tell me?
It's-uh-[CITY], I believe. My name is John Smith.
Now can you tell me what happened prior to passing out and how you feel now? Don't worry about getting everything right-just tell me what you remember.
I snorted something earlier. That's all I remember before I passed out. Now I feel better once Narcan was given, but I'm still a little out of it.
He has a pulse rate of 118-strong and regular, same as the first noted in prehospital vitals.
Respiratory rate is 16, shallow with BVM assist and in room air currently, as well as shallow without it, according to prior readings.
Pre-arrival vitals show a blood pressure of 125/69 and stable so far, no significant changes so far observed.
Oxygen saturation in situ was 61% on room air, now up to 89% on BVM at 15LPM and on additional 4LPM nasal cannula for maintenance after Narcan.
4-lead ECG on record shows sinus tachycardia running consistently, no significant changes so far detected while monitoring.
Blood glucose initially measured at 366, then a second reading came back as 0. Possibly due to exposure of a previously administered Narcan vial by chance.
Patient has moved from a GCS of 3 to now being alert and oriented to person and place, 3/4 orientation regained per post-Narcan observation from EKG and interventions section report.

IV access gained via a 16g in left AC after first attempt, flushed with 10mL saline before and after Narcan administration at appropriate dose to ensure effectiveness per standing order and protocol 7-1.

Administration of 1mg Narcan via IV push followed by another 10mL saline flush observed successful and subsequent spontaneous breathing improved to about 20 per minute post-dose according to field reports.

Oxygen has been administered through nasal cannula at 4LPM to help maintain airway support and stable vital signs, as per additional documentation under the medication and intervention section citing O2 use post Narcan.

ETCO2 monitoring was applied via nasal cannula and reading 52 observed during initial transport prep according to supplied field notes on EKG and interventions performed post Narcan-no significant changes noticed in carbon dioxide levels observed at this point in time for now related to documented procedure start steps.

Opioid Overdose Protocol 7-1 activated and Narcan administered per standing order and known protocols specific to the field response narrative covered in the ePCR documentation already mentioned and validated of use in the processes agreed upon for this case initially.
Let's re-check the IV line for secure placement and assess O2 flow before initiating transportation to [COMPANY_NAME] per routine pre-transport.
IV and O2 lines remain secure without signs of leakage or dislodgement consistent with pre-transport criteria observed.
Stretcher readiness now confirmed for transport. All airway support and monitoring equipment are in place for transport.
Transport destination is [COMPANY_NAME] for further observation and hospital management. Patient agrees with destination choice.
Stretcher is relocated at 90 degrees and secure for loading into ambulance for transport now.
Institutional transport will be non-emergent. Patient is stable for loading and transport now under current conditions.
Patient is transferring to [COMPANY_NAME]-Bed 7. Full care and report to be transferred to staff RN upon station arrival.
Narcan was effective-airway, breathing, and vitals stable with mention of an IV line, O2, and ECG-secure setup post bathing and dressing change by receiving nurse.
Documentation does include prior read vitals, use of Narcan, 16g IV, and O2 administration at 4LPM nasal cannula as outlined for transition handoff to RN as informed via the report.
Loading secured with 5 point restraint harness for transport once stretcher is loaded and ready for rolling in as noted during pre-transport steps.
[COMPANY_NAME] accepted care. Report was given to the oncoming nurse for proper continuity of care at the receiving medical center.
The patient currently has stable responsiveness and is on continuous observation for any post-Narcan deterioration.
Vital signs, including SpO2 and BP, have been maintained throughout transport time.
Hey, did we note his pupil size yet?
Yes, they were pinpoint bilaterally, as we previously identified from the patient report.
And he's alert and oriented now, with improved breathing and vitals.
Yes, though pinpoint pupils were previously noted, as we just said.
